column1: With Extant Fragments, Myth Cycles, and Archaeological Evidence

This index catalogs the major and minor hero cults across the Greek, Thracian, Anatolian, Steppe, and related Eurasian worlds, focusing on historical reconstruction using existing literary fragments, archaeological finds, and myth cycles.
Sources include epic cycles, inscriptions, shrine remains, votive offerings, and ancient literary references.
Citations are provided where possible.
I. Greek Hero Cults (Mainland & Colonies)
II. Steppe, Thracian, and Eurasian Hero Cults
III. Anatolian, Hellenistic, and Syncretic Hero Cults
IV. Western and Celtic Hero Cults
V. Key Features of Hero Cults (Across Traditions)
Locality: Hero cults are typically local, centered on tombs, shrines, or mounds associated with the hero18.
Chthonic Rituals: Offerings (libations, sacrifices) are chthonic, often at night, and not shared with the living1.
Political Use: Cults often used for political legitimation (e.g., Cleisthenes’ Athenian demes, Spartan use of Orestes’ bones)1.
Myth-Epic Influence: Epic cycles (Iliad, Odyssey, Argonautica, Theban cycle) heavily influenced which figures became cult heroes157.
Fragmentary Evidence: Many cults known only from archaeological finds (votive plaques, tumuli, inscriptions) or brief literary mentions5679.
VI. Major Extant Fragments & Literary Sources
Epic Cycles: Iliad, Odyssey, Theban Cycle, Argonautica (Apollonius Rhodius), Homeric Hymns, Orphic Hymns
Inscriptions & Archaeology: Dedication plaques, stelae (e.g., Menelaion at Sparta), kurgan burials (Scythian/Sarmatian), reliefs (Thracian Horseman)
Literary References: Pausanias, Herodotus, Pindar, Sophocles, Apollonius, Strabo, local chronicles
Orphic Gold Tablets: Funerary lamellae with instructions for the afterlife, mainly from Magna Graecia and Thrace
VII. Digital Resources for Further Study
Perseus Digital Library: Greek texts, inscriptions, archaeological reports
Attalus: Greek and Roman sources, events, inscriptions
